meta.description: CGI/IUGS controlled vocabulary of contact types used in GeoSciML Contact descriptions. Contacts separate adjacent geological units and may be depositional, erosional, intrusive, tectonic, or metamorphic in origin.

concepts.definition: A surface or narrow zone separating two geological units of different lithology, age, or origin. The fundamental unit of geological mapping. Top concept of the contact type vocabulary.
A contact between geological units of different lithological character. The boundary may be gradational or sharp and does not necessarily imply a time gap or structural event.
A contact between strata that have a defined stratigraphic relationship — either conformable (continuous deposition) or unconformable (depositional break or erosion surface).
A stratigraphic contact where the beds above and below are parallel and there is no significant break in sedimentation. Indicates continuous or near-continuous deposition.

A stratigraphic contact representing a significant time gap (hiatus) in the rock record, caused by erosion or non-deposition. The unconformity surface records a period of geological history not represented by strata.

An unconformity in which younger strata overlie an erosion surface on tilted or folded older rocks. The beds above and below are not parallel. Represents a cycle of deposition, deformation, erosion, and renewed deposition.
An unconformity in which the bedding planes above and below are parallel but an erosion surface separates them, representing a period of erosion or non-deposition without tilting.

An unconformity where stratified sedimentary rocks overlie igneous or metamorphic rocks. The contact represents a period of uplift, erosion of the older crystalline basement, and subsequent burial by sediments.
An unconformity in which the strata above and below are parallel and the contact appears conformable but represents a significant time gap identified by paleontological or geochemical evidence.
A contact between an intrusive igneous rock body (e.g., dyke, sill, pluton) and the country rock it has intruded. May show chilled margins, baked aureoles, and cross-cutting relationships.
A contact bounding a metamorphic unit, separating rocks of different metamorphic grade or between metamorphic and non-metamorphic rocks. May be gradational (isogrades) or sharp (thrust-related).
A contact produced by faulting, where rock units on either side have been juxtaposed by tectonic displacement rather than by original depositional or intrusive relationships.
